records.ety: [F. descente, fr. descendre; like vente, from vendre. See Descend.]

defs: The act of descending, or passing downward; change of place from higher to lower.
Incursion; sudden attack; especially, hostile invasion from sea; -- often followed by upon or on; as, to make a descent upon the enemy.
quotes: The United Provinces . . . ordered public prayer to God, when they feared that the French and English fleets would make a descent upon their coasts.

defs: Progress downward, as in station, virtue, as in station, virtue, and the like, from a higher to a lower state, from a higher to a lower state, from the more to the less important, from the better to the worse, etc.
Derivation, as from an ancestor; procedure by generation; lineage; birth; extraction.
Transmission of an estate by inheritance, usually, but not necessarily, in the descending line; title to inherit an estate by reason of consanguinity.
Inclination downward; a descending way; inclined or sloping surface; declivity; slope; as, a steep descent.
That which is descended; descendants; issue.
quotes: If care of our descent perplex us most, Which must be born to certain woe.

defs: A step or remove downward in any scale of gradation; a degree in the scale of genealogy; a generation.
quotes: No man living is a thousand descents removed from Adam himself.
And from the extremest upward of thy head, To the descent and dust below thy foot.

defs: A passing from a higher to a lower tone.
